45300

March 31, 1976

Walter N. Anderson, City
Attorney
1700 West 162nd Street
Gardena, California 90247

Dear Mr. Anderson:

Your letter of March 5, 1976, to Robert Stern, has been referred to me for a response. I have reviewed the Gardena ordinance relating to campaign disclosures and offer a few preliminary thoughts.

First, as I am sure you're aware, the Political Reform Act, Government Code Section 81000 et seq. applies to local elections. Municipalities may impose additional requirements on any person if the requirements do not prevent the person from complying with [the Political Reform Act]. Government Code Section 81013. Although the ordinance in its present form does not appear to contain any provisions that would prevent a person from complying with the Political Reform Act, you should be aware of the standard imposed by Section 81013 as you interpret and amend the ordinance.

It would appear that the limitation imposed by Section 4-16.04 cannot stand in light of the Buckley decision.

"A primary effect of these expenditure limitations is to restrict the quantity of campaign speech by individuals, groups, and candidates. The restrictions, while neutral as to the ideas expressed, limit political expression at the core of our electoral process and of First Amendment freedom." 96 S.Ct. 612, 644 (1976)–

Similarly, it does appear that the limitation on the amount that a candidate may contribute to his own campaign (Sec. 4-16.03 (2)) can be interpreted in a way that is consistent with Buckley.

"The candidate, no less than any other person has a First Amendment right to engage in the discussion of public issues and vigorously and tirelessly to advocate his own election."... The primary governmental interest served by the Act, the prevention


Due to the severely faded and degraded condition of this document, with text appearing in reverse/mirror image or heavily worn typewriter impressions, I cannot reliably transcribe the full content while adhering to the requirement of verbatim accuracy. The page quality makes it impossible to definitively distinguish between actual text, artifacts, and illegible sections without significant guessing.

[Illegible - document too faded/degraded to transcribe accurately]

Walter N. Anderson
March 31, 1976
Page Two

of actual and apparent corruption on the political process,
does not support the limitation of the candidate's expenditure
of his own funds." 96 S.Ct. 612, 651 (1976). These are the
only two provisions of the ordinance that would seem to be
completely invalidated by the Buckley decision.

I hope this information will be of assistance.

Sincerely,

Natalie E. West
Staff Counsel

[Due to the poor quality, faintness, and inverted orientation of the document, I cannot reliably transcribe the specific text content without significant uncertainty.]

1700 WEST 162ND STREET/GARDENA, CALIFORNIA 90247/(213) 327-0220

March 5th, 1976

Robert Stern, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street Building
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Stern:

I am writing at the request of Councilman Mas Fukai,

who appeared in Sacramento and testified before one of

the committees relating to Fair Practices Commission

matters.

Of particular interest to Councilman Fukai is the fact

that our Ordinance No. 1022, which was the subject of

a referendum and by a vote of 3-2 of the City Council,

was placed on the ballot for the election of March 2nd,

1976. Although there was substantial opposition to this

Ordinance, particularly by our six licensed card clubs

and a heavily financed campaign was leveled in opposition

to Ordinance No. 1022, Councilman Fukai wanted you to

know that the voters of the City of Gardena approved

Measure A, the campaign limitation expenditure ordinance


Page 2.

by a Yes vote of 3,170 as against a No vote of 1,809.

A copy of the ballot used, together with an unofficial tally

of results for the entire election, is enclosed herewith for

your information.

Specifically, Councilman Fukai asked that I write you con-

cerning your views and opinions as to the validity of the

Ordinance as now constituted. I, of course, feel that some

of the provisions are modified by the decision of the

Supreme Court of the United States in Buckley vs. Valco

in 96 Supreme Court Reporter, page 612 with regard to the

fact that the candidate may make an unlimited expenditure

of his own funds for his own campaign.

Any suggestions or views you may have in connection with

Ordinance No. 1022 would be very much appreciated.

Thanking you for your interest,

Very truly yours,

WNA:gb                           Walter N. Anderson
Enclosures                        City Attorney

cc: Councilman Mas Fukai


UNOFFICIAL RESULTS

CITY OF GARDENA
GENERAL MUNICIPAL ELECTION
MARCH 2, 1976

44 PRECINCTS OUT OF 44 REPORTED
(INCLUDES ABSENTEE BALLOTS)

VOTES RECEIVED

FOR MAYOR:

AL DEFILIPPO                                1993
EDMOND J. (ED) RUSS                          2872

FOR MEMBER OF THE CITY COUNCIL:

VINCENT H. OKAMOTO                           3406
MASARU (MAS) ODOI                            2442
RICHARD ARIAS                                1733
A. WILLIAM GIBSON                            2712

FOR CITY CLERK:

DORIS DIAMOND BANKUS                         3510
MARY HOLDEFER                                837
GENAY HORTON                                 1373

FOR CITY TREASURER:

GEORGE KOBAYASHI                             2423
LOU LAUZON                                   1177
BOB ARNOLD                                   1153

MEASURE A.                YES                 3170
                         NO                  1809

TOTAL VOTES CAST: _____________


ORDINANCE NO. 1022

AN ORDINANCE OF THE CITY OF GARDENA,

CALIFORNIA, ADDING CHAPTER 16 TO

TITLE 4 OF THE GARDENA MUNICIPAL CODE,

RELATING TO CONTROL OF MUNICIPAL

ELECTION CAMPAIGN CONTRIBUTIONS AND

EXPENDITURES.

THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF GARDENA,

CALIFORNIA, DOES ORDAIN AS FOLLOWS:

SECTION 1. AMENDMENT. That Chapter 16 is hereby

added to Title 4 of the Gardena Municipal Code, to read as follows:

"CHAPTER 16

"ELECTION CAMPAIGN CONTRIBUTIONS
AND EXPENDITURES

"Sec. 4-16.01. Purpose and Intent.

"The problem of ever-increasing campaign

expenditures has become a serious, if not disastrous,

reality of American politics, and campaigns in the City

of Gardena are not excepted. Incidental to the high

cost of election campaigning is the problem of

improper influence, real or potential, exercised by

campaign contributors over elected officials.

"It is the purpose and intent of the City Council of

the City of Gardena, in enacting this ordinance, to place

realistic and enforceable limits on the amounts which

may be spent for political campaigning in municipal

elections; to place realistic and enforceable limits on

the amounts individuals may contribute to political

campaigns in municipal elections; to insure and promote

-1-


integrity, honesty and fairness in the decisions of
public policy; to insure that funds and services
donated to a candidate or campaign committee are
used solely for lawful campaign purposes; to insure
a level of discussion of public issues adequate for a
viable political campaign and to provide opportunity
for all citizens to become candidates for public
office unhindered by a need to meet exorbitant
campaign costs; to require a full public disclosure
of campaign contributions received, the names and
addresses of contributors to such campaigns, and
the purpose and amounts of expenditures in political
campaigns in municipal elections; and to provide
full and fair enforcement of all the provisions of
this chapter.

"In seeking to establish such realistic limitations
on campaign costs, it is the intent of the City Council
to promote a broader and more open participation
by all citizens in the electoral process and it is in
no way intended that such limitations should act to
deprive or restrict any citizen in the exercise of
his rights guaranteed under the First and Fourteenth
Amendments of the United States Constitution.

"The City Council finds, in this regard, that
municipal elections constitute municipal affairs, and
that the reasonable regulation thereof as herein set
forth is permitted by Section 22808 of the Elections
Code of the State of California.

"Sec. 4-16.02. Definitions.

"Whenever in this chapter the following words

-2-


or phrases are used, they shall mean:

"(1) 'Campaign statement' shall mean itemized

statement prepared in duplicate by a candidate and

by the treasurer of each committee showing, in

addition to matters otherwise required by law, the

following information:

"(a) The full name, complete mailing address

and occupation, if any, of any person who paid, loaned,

contributed or otherwise furnished Fifty Dollars

($50.00) or more to the candidate or treasurer for

the candidate or to each committee as defined in this

chapter, for the use of such candidate or such treasurer,

directly or indirectly, in aid of the candidate's election,

and the amount, in detail, of such money each such

person paid, loaned, contributed, or otherwise

furnished.

"(b) The purchase of any tickets, cumulatively

totaling Fifty Dollars ($50.00) or more for any fund-

raising event regardless of number purchased, value

of each ticket, or frequency of purchase, is subject

to the provisions of paragraph (a) above.

"(c) Donation of Fifty Dollars ($50.00) or

more to any 'kitty' at any campaign event is subject

to the provisions of paragraph (a) above.

"(d) All expenditures in excess of Fifty

Dollars ($50.00) shall be itemized in detail with

the amounts and names of persons and/or concerns

where the monies were expended.

"(2) 'Candidate' shall mean any individual listed

on the ballot for nomination for, or election to, any

-3-


City office, or who has otherwise taken affirmative action to seek nomination or election to public
office.

"(3) 'Committee' shall mean:

"(a) Any person or combination of two
or more persons acting jointly and organized for the
purpose or charged with the duty of conducting or
aiding the election campaign, including fund-raising
events, of any candidate for municipal office of the
City;

"(b) Any committee or group of persons
aiding directly or indirectly any committee as defined
in (a) above, whether or not originally organized for
election campaigns;

"(c) Any person or combination of two or
more persons acting jointly in opposition to a
candidate for municipal office.

"(4) 'Contribution' shall mean a gift, subscription,
loan, advance, deposit, pledge, contract, agreement
or promise of money or anything of value or other
obligation, whether or not legally enforceable, made
directly or indirectly in aid of, or in opposition to,
the nomination or election or recall of one or more
candidates.

"The term 'contribution' includes the purchase
of tickets for events such as dinners, luncheons, rallies
and similar fund-raising events; a candidate's or
committee's own money or property used on behalf of
a candidate; the granting to a candidate or committee
of discounts or rebates not available to the general public;

-4-


and payments for the services of any person serving

in behalf of a candidate or committee, when such

payments are not made from contributions the

candidate or committee otherwise must report under

the terms of this chapter.

"The term 'contribution' further includes

any transfer, gift, loan, advance, deposit, pledge,

contract, agreement or promise of money or anything

of value or other obligation, whether or not legally

enforceable, received directly or indirectly by a

committee from another committee.

"The term 'contribution' shall not include a

gift of service or labor when volunteered by the person

performing the service or labor, but shall include a

gift of service or labor paid for by a person other

than the person performing the service or labor.

"The term 'contribution' shall not include

the cost of an event held in honor or behalf of a

candidate or committee when the total cost of the

event amounts to no more than Two Hundred Dollars

($200.00) and when the event is not held for the

purpose of obtaining contributions to the candidate

or committee.

"Notwithstanding the foregoing definition

of 'contribution', the term does not include volunteer

personal services or payments made by an individual

for the individual's own travel expenses, if such

payments are made voluntarily without any under-

standing or agreement that these shall be, directly

or indirectly, repaid to the individual.

-5-

First, as I am sure you're aware, the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000 et seq., applies to local elections. Municipalities may "impose additional requirements on any person if the requirements do not prevent the person from complying with [the Political Reform Act]". Government Code Section 81013. Although the ordinance in its present form does not appear to contain any provisions that would prevent a person from complying with the Political Reform Act, you should be aware of the standard imposed by Section 81013 as you interpret and amend the ordinance. It would appear that the limitation imposed by Section 4-16.04 cannot stand in light of the Buckley decision. "A primary effect of these expenditure limitations is to restrict the quantity of campaign speech by individuals, groups, and candidates. The restrictions, while neutral as to the ideas expressed, limit political expression 'at the core of our electoral process and of First Amendment freedom'." 96 S.Ct. 612, 644 (1976). Similarly, it does appear that the limitation on the amount that a candidate may contribute to his own campaign (Sec. 4-16.03 (2)) can be interpreted in a way that is consistent with Buckley. "The candidate, no less than any other person has a First Amendment right to engage in the discussion of public issues and vigorously and tirelessly to advocate his own election."...
